subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Look around your home for a moment. When your mother pours milk into a warm pot and it slowly turns into thick, creamy yoghurt, something has changed. When your father lights the charcoal in the stove and the black lumps glow red and turn to grey ash, something has changed. When you drop a spoon of sugar into your hot shaah (tea) and stir until it disappears, something has changed. Every single day, all around you, materials are changing. Some of these changes can be undone, and some can never be undone again. This unit is all about understanding why materials change, how we can tell a change has happened, and whether we can get the original material back.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 6 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Tell the difference between a reversible change and an irreversible change, and give clear examples of each from your own life.
Explain the difference between a physical change and a chemical change, and describe the evidence that a new material has been made.
Describe what mixtures and solutions are, and how they are different from pure substances.
Investigate and choose the right method to separate a mixture, such as filtering, evaporating, sieving, or using a magnet.
Use particle theory - the idea that everything is made of tiny particles - to explain melting, dissolving, and other changes.
Identify the factors that make a solid dissolve faster, such as heat, stirring, and grinding into smaller pieces.
Connect what you learn to real life in your community, from rusting roofs to making yoghurt and collecting salt.

Before we begin, here are the important words for this unit. Do not try to memorise them all at once. You will meet each one many times, and they will start to feel familiar. Keep coming back to this table whenever you forget a word.
A change that can be undone, so you can get the original material back.

concepts.title: Material Changes
concepts.explanation: Look around your home for a moment. When your mother pours milk into a warm pot and it slowly turns into thick, creamy yoghurt, something has changed. When your father lights the charcoal in the stove and the black lumps glow red and turn to grey ash, something has changed. When you drop a spoon of sugar into your hot shaah (tea) and stir until it disappears, something has changed. Every single day, all around you, materials are changing. Some of these changes can be undone, and some can never be undone again.…
concepts.example: Scientists call this topic material changes. It is one of the most useful ideas in all of science, because it helps you understand cooking, cleaning, rusting metal, burning fuel, making medicine, purifying water, and…

concepts.title: Material Changes — part 2
concepts.explanation: Scientists call this topic material changes. It is one of the most useful ideas in all of science, because it helps you understand cooking, cleaning, rusting metal, burning fuel, making medicine, purifying water, and even how the food in your stomach is broken down. By the end of this unit you will be able to look at almost any change in the kitchen, in the workshop, or out on the coast and explain it like a scientist.
concepts.example: Here in East Africa, material changes shape daily life in special ways. On the humid Indian Ocean coast near Mombasa, Berbera, and Kismaayo, the salty sea air makes metal roofs and tools go rusty and orange. In dry…

concepts.title: Material Changes — part 3
concepts.explanation: Here in East Africa, material changes shape daily life in special ways. On the humid Indian Ocean coast near Mombasa, Berbera, and Kismaayo, the salty sea air makes metal roofs and tools go rusty and orange. In dry inland towns like Garissa and Hargeisa, people spread seawater or salty water in shallow pans and let the strong sun evaporate it away to collect pure salt. When Halima bakes canjeero on a hot pan, the runny batter becomes soft, spongy bread that can never turn back into batter. All of these are…
concepts.example: As Muslims, we are taught to be khalifah - careful guardians of the earth that Allah created. Understanding how materials change helps us waste less, keep water and air clean, and use fuel like charcoal and firewood…

concepts.title: Material Changes — part 4
concepts.explanation: As Muslims, we are taught to be khalifah - careful guardians of the earth that Allah created. Understanding how materials change helps us waste less, keep water and air clean, and use fuel like charcoal and firewood wisely. When you observe a change carefully and ask why it happened, you are reflecting on the order and wisdom in creation. Curiosity is a beautiful part of learning.
concepts.example: [AI] Ask Your AI Tutor Try starting your unit by asking your AI tutor: "Give me three examples of material changes I can find in my own kitchen right now, and tell me if each one can be undone." Then go and check each…

concepts.title: Material Changes — part 5
concepts.explanation: [AI] Ask Your AI Tutor Try starting your unit by asking your AI tutor: "Give me three examples of material changes I can find in my own kitchen right now, and tell me if each one can be undone." Then go and check each one for yourself. Come back and tell your tutor what you found.
concepts.example: Tell the difference between a reversible change and an irreversible change, and give clear examples of each from your own life.

concepts.title: Material Changes — part 6
concepts.explanation: Tell the difference between a reversible change and an irreversible change, and give clear examples of each from your own life.
concepts.example: Look around your home for a moment. When your mother pours milk into a warm pot and it slowly turns into thick, creamy yoghurt, something has changed. When your father lights the charcoal in the stove and the black…

explorations.title: The Fizzing Reaction (Vinegar and Baking Soda)
explorations.context: To see the evidence of an irreversible chemical change when vinegar meets baking soda.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write down what you think will happen when you pour vinegar onto baking soda. Do you think you could get your vinegar and baking soda back afterwards? Why or why not?
explorations.answer: What gas do you think made all the bubbles? (Hint: it is the same gas you breathe out.) List two pieces of evidence that a chemical change happened. Could you separate the mixture back into vinegar and baking soda? What does this tell you?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To see the evidence of an irreversible chemical change when vinegar meets baking soda.

explorations.title: Growing Rust (Iron Nails Investigation)
explorations.context: To find out what iron needs in order to rust, just like the metal roofs and tools along the coast.
explorations.prompt: Which nail do you think will rust the most - the dry one, the one in water, or the one kept away from air? Write your prediction and your reason.
explorations.answer: Which nail rusted the most? Which rusted the least? Iron needs two things to rust. Based on your results, what are they? Why did the oil-covered nail stay shiny? How does this explain why people oil their tools? If you added salt to Cup B, did it rust faster? What does this tell you about metal…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out what iron needs in order to rust, just like the metal roofs and tools along the coast.

explorations.title: The Dissolving Speed Race
explorations.context: To find out whether heat, stirring, and grinding really do make sugar dissolve faster.
explorations.prompt: Do you think a sugar cube or lump will dissolve faster in hot water or cold water? Write your prediction.
explorations.answer: Which dissolved sugar faster - warm or cold water? Explain why using particles. Did stirring make a difference? Why do you think that is? Why does ground sugar dissolve faster than a whole lump? Is dissolving a reversible or irreversible change? How could you prove it?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out whether heat, stirring, and grinding really do make sugar dissolve faster.

explorations.title: Getting Salt Back From Salt Water
explorations.context: To prove that dissolving is reversible by separating salt back out of salt water, just as coastal people collect sea salt.
explorations.prompt: When the water evaporates, what do you think will be left in the dish? Write your prediction.
explorations.answer: Where did the water go? What is this process called? What was left behind in the dish, and where did it come from? Does this prove dissolving is reversible? Explain your answer. How is your dish like the salt pans used near Berbera and the coast?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To prove that dissolving is reversible by separating salt back out of salt water, just as coastal people collect sea salt.

explorations.title: Cleaning Muddy Water by Filtering
explorations.context: To use filtration to separate solid dirt from water and see how communities clean muddy river water.
explorations.prompt: Will filtering remove the mud? Do you think the filtered water will be safe to drink straight away? Write your ideas.
explorations.answer: What did the filter trap, and what passed through it? Why does filtering remove mud but not dissolved salt? The water looks cleaner, but why is it still not safe to drink until it is boiled? How is this like the way people clean water from a muddy river?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To use filtration to separate solid dirt from water and see how communities clean muddy river water.

methods.title: The Fizzing Reaction (Vinegar and Baking Soda)
methods.example: To see the evidence of an irreversible chemical change when vinegar meets baking soda.
steps: Put the two spoons of baking soda into the bottom of the tall glass.
Look closely at the baking soda and the vinegar. Note their colour and whether they are solid or liquid.
Slowly pour the vinegar onto the baking soda and step back a little.
Watch carefully. Note what happens straight away and for the next minute.
Once the fizzing stops, look at what is left in the glass.

methods.title: Growing Rust (Iron Nails Investigation)
methods.example: To find out what iron needs in order to rust, just like the metal roofs and tools along the coast.
steps: Label your three cups A, B, and C.
Cup A: leave a nail dry with nothing added, open to the air.
Cup B: put a nail in and cover it completely with water.
Cup C: put a nail in and cover it completely with cooking oil so no air can reach it.
For an extra test, add a pinch of salt to Cup B to copy the salty coastal air.

methods.title: The Dissolving Speed Race
methods.example: To find out whether heat, stirring, and grinding really do make sugar dissolve faster.
steps: Fill one glass with cold water and one with the same amount of warm water.
At the same moment, drop one level spoon of sugar into each glass. Do not stir.
Time how long the sugar takes to fully disappear in each glass and record it.
Now repeat with two fresh glasses of cold water. In one, stir hard; in the other, do not stir. Time both.
For a final test, compare a whole sugar lump against the same amount of ground-up sugar in two glasses of cold water.

methods.title: Getting Salt Back From Salt Water
methods.example: To prove that dissolving is reversible by separating salt back out of salt water, just as coastal people collect sea salt.
steps: Stir the salt into the warm water until it completely dissolves and you cannot see it any more.
Look at the water - it should be clear. The salt is now a solution.
Pour a thin layer of the salt water into the shallow dish.
Place the dish in a sunny, safe spot where it will not be disturbed.
Leave it for one to three days and check it each day as the water slowly evaporates.

methods.title: Cleaning Muddy Water by Filtering
methods.example: To use filtration to separate solid dirt from water and see how communities clean muddy river water.
steps: Place the cloth or filter inside the funnel, and sit the funnel in the empty cup.
For a better filter, add a layer of clean sand and small stones on top of the cloth.
Slowly pour the muddy water into the filter and watch what drips through.
Compare the water that comes out with the muddy water you started with.
Look at what is left behind on the cloth and sand.

workedExamples.title: Planning a Separation
workedExamples.prompt: Let us work through a separation problem together, step by step, the way a scientist would.
workedExamples.solution: Problem: Zahra has accidentally mixed together a spoon of sand, a spoon of salt, and some small iron filings in one bowl. She wants to separate all three back into clean, separate piles. How can she do it? Step 1 - Look at the properties. Iron is magnetic; sand and salt are not. Salt dissolves in water; sand and iron do not. These two facts are the keys to the whole plan. Step 2 - Remove the iron first. Move a magnet over the dry mixture. The iron filings jump to the magnet, and Zahra lifts them clean away. Now…

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: Which is an irreversible change? (a) Freezing water (b) Dissolving sugar (c) Baking canjeero (d) Melting wax
workedExamples.solution: (c) Baking canjeero. Cooking makes a new material that cannot turn back into batter. Freezing, dissolving, and melting can all be reversed.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: When salt dissolves in water, the salt: (a) disappears forever (b) turns into a new material (c) spreads out between the water particles (d) reacts to make a gas
workedExamples.solution: (c) It spreads out between the water particles. The salt is still there, hidden among the water particles, which is why the water tastes salty and the salt returns when the water evaporates.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: In a cup of sweet shaah, the sugar is the: (a) solvent (b) solute (c) solution (d) mixture
workedExamples.solution: (b) Solute. The solute is the solid that dissolves - here, the sugar.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: In salt water, the water is the: (a) solute (b) solvent (c) solid (d) filter
workedExamples.solution: (b) Solvent. The solvent is the liquid that does the dissolving - here, the water.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Which tool would separate iron filings from sand? (a) A sieve (b) A filter (c) A magnet (d) Evaporation
workedExamples.solution: (c) A magnet. Iron is magnetic, so a magnet pulls the iron filings out while leaving the sand behind.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: Which method separates salt from salt water? (a) Filtering (b) Using a magnet (c) Evaporation (d) Sieving
workedExamples.solution: (c) Evaporation. Heating the salt water lets the water escape as gas and leaves the salt behind. A magnet and sieve would not work, and a filter cannot catch dissolved salt.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: Iron needs which two things to rust? (a) Water and oxygen (b) Heat and light (c) Salt and sugar (d) Oil and air
workedExamples.solution: (a) Water and oxygen. Iron rusts only when both water and oxygen from the air can reach it.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a reversible change? (a) Burning charcoal (b) Melting ghee (c) Frying an egg (d) Rusting a nail
practice.answer: (b) Melting ghee. Melting only changes the state; the ghee can be cooled back to solid, so it is reversible. The others all make new materials.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which is an irreversible change? (a) Freezing water (b) Dissolving sugar (c) Baking canjeero (d) Melting wax
practice.answer: (c) Baking canjeero. Cooking makes a new material that cannot turn back into batter. Freezing, dissolving, and melting can all be reversed.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: When salt dissolves in water, the salt: (a) disappears forever (b) turns into a new material (c) spreads out between the water particles (d) reacts to make a gas
practice.answer: (c) It spreads out between the water particles. The salt is still there, hidden among the water particles, which is why the water tastes salty and the salt returns when the water evaporates.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: In a cup of sweet shaah, the sugar is the: (a) solvent (b) solute (c) solution (d) mixture
practice.answer: (b) Solute. The solute is the solid that dissolves - here, the sugar.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: In salt water, the water is the: (a) solute (b) solvent (c) solid (d) filter
practice.answer: (b) Solvent. The solvent is the liquid that does the dissolving - here, the water.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which tool would separate iron filings from sand? (a) A sieve (b) A filter (c) A magnet (d) Evaporation
practice.answer: (c) A magnet. Iron is magnetic, so a magnet pulls the iron filings out while leaving the sand behind.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which method separates salt from salt water? (a) Filtering (b) Using a magnet (c) Evaporation (d) Sieving
practice.answer: (c) Evaporation. Heating the salt water lets the water escape as gas and leaves the salt behind. A magnet and sieve would not work, and a filter cannot catch dissolved salt.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Iron needs which two things to rust? (a) Water and oxygen (b) Heat and light (c) Salt and sugar (d) Oil and air
practice.answer: (a) Water and oxygen. Iron rusts only when both water and oxygen from the air can reach it.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a clue that a chemical change has happened? (a) A new colour appears (b) A gas is made (c) Heat is given out (d) All of these
practice.answer: (d) All of these. A new colour, a new gas, and heat or light are all clues that a chemical change has happened.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which is a pure substance? (a) Seawater (b) Air (c) Clean water (d) Rice with stones
practice.answer: (c) Clean water. Pure water is made of only one kind of material. Seawater, air, and rice with stones are all mixtures.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which makes sugar dissolve faster? (a) Colder water (b) Bigger lumps (c) Stirring (d) Less water
practice.answer: (c) Stirring. Stirring, heating, and using smaller pieces all speed up dissolving.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A mixture where you cannot see the separate parts, like salt water, is called: (a) heterogeneous (b) homogeneous (c) irreversible (d) magnetic
practice.answer: (b) Homogeneous. In a homogeneous mixture the parts are spread evenly and cannot be seen separately, like salt water.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: The Fizzing Reaction (Vinegar and Baking Soda)
activities.materials: Two spoons of baking soda (bicarbonate of soda); Half a cup of vinegar; A tall clear glass or jar; A small spoon
steps: Put the two spoons of baking soda into the bottom of the tall glass.
Look closely at the baking soda and the vinegar. Note their colour and whether they are solid or liquid.
Slowly pour the vinegar onto the baking soda and step back a little.
Watch carefully. Note what happens straight away and for the next minute.
Once the fizzing stops, look at what is left in the glass.

activities.title: Growing Rust (Iron Nails Investigation)
activities.materials: Three clean iron nails (steel nails work well); Three small jars or cups; Water; Cooking oil (a few spoonfuls); A little salt (optional, to copy sea air)
steps: Label your three cups A, B, and C.
Cup A: leave a nail dry with nothing added, open to the air.
Cup B: put a nail in and cover it completely with water.
Cup C: put a nail in and cover it completely with cooking oil so no air can reach it.
For an extra test, add a pinch of salt to Cup B to copy the salty coastal air.

activities.title: The Dissolving Speed Race
activities.materials: Sugar (loose sugar and, if you have them, a couple of sugar lumps); Two clear glasses; Cold water and warm water (ask an adult to warm it); A spoon for stirring; A clock or timer
steps: Fill one glass with cold water and one with the same amount of warm water.
At the same moment, drop one level spoon of sugar into each glass. Do not stir.
Time how long the sugar takes to fully disappear in each glass and record it.
Now repeat with two fresh glasses of cold water. In one, stir hard; in the other, do not stir. Time both.
For a final test, compare a whole sugar lump against the same amount of ground-up sugar in two glasses of cold water.

activities.title: Getting Salt Back From Salt Water
activities.materials: A spoon of salt; Warm water in a cup; A dark-coloured shallow dish or plate; A sunny windowsill or warm, safe spot
steps: Stir the salt into the warm water until it completely dissolves and you cannot see it any more.
Look at the water - it should be clear. The salt is now a solution.
Pour a thin layer of the salt water into the shallow dish.
Place the dish in a sunny, safe spot where it will not be disturbed.
Leave it for one to three days and check it each day as the water slowly evaporates.

activities.title: Cleaning Muddy Water by Filtering
activities.materials: A cup of muddy water (stir a little clean soil or sand into water); A clean cloth or a coffee filter; An empty cup or jar; A funnel or the top half of a cut plastic bottle (ask an adult to cut it); Optional: clean sand and small clean stones
steps: Place the cloth or filter inside the funnel, and sit the funnel in the empty cup.
For a better filter, add a layer of clean sand and small stones on top of the cloth.
Slowly pour the muddy water into the filter and watch what drips through.
Compare the water that comes out with the muddy water you started with.
Look at what is left behind on the cloth and sand.

activities.title: Observe: Material Changes — part 6
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of material changes — part 6 around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

terms: A change that can be undone to get the original material back.
A change that cannot be undone; a new material is usually made.
A change of shape or state with no new material made.
A change that makes one or more new materials.
Materials mixed but not chemically joined; can be separated.
A mixture where a solid has dissolved evenly and cannot be seen.
The liquid that does the dissolving.
How much solute can dissolve in a solvent.
A solution that cannot hold any more dissolved solid.
Separating an undissolved solid from a liquid using a filter.
A liquid turning to gas, leaving a dissolved solid behind.

vocabulary.meaning: A change that can be undone to get the original material back.
vocabulary.example: Tell the difference between a reversible change and an irreversible change, and give clear examples of each from your own life.

vocabulary.meaning: A change that cannot be undone; a new material is usually made.
vocabulary.example: Tell the difference between a reversible change and an irreversible change, and give clear examples of each from your own life.

vocabulary.meaning: A change of shape or state with no new material made.
vocabulary.example: Explain the difference between a physical change and a chemical change, and describe the evidence that a new material has been made.

vocabulary.meaning: A change that makes one or more new materials.
vocabulary.example: Explain the difference between a physical change and a chemical change, and describe the evidence that a new material has been made.

vocabulary.meaning: Materials mixed but not chemically joined; can be separated.
vocabulary.example: Investigate and choose the right method to separate a mixture, such as filtering, evaporating, sieving, or using a magnet.

vocabulary.meaning: A mixture where a solid has dissolved evenly and cannot be seen.
vocabulary.example: When a solute spreads out completely into a solvent to make a solution.
When a solute spreads out completely into a solvent to make a solution.

vocabulary.meaning: The liquid that does the dissolving.
vocabulary.example: When a solute spreads out completely into a solvent to make a solution.

vocabulary.meaning: How much solute can dissolve in a solvent.
vocabulary.example: Sugar has high solubility in water; sand has almost none.

vocabulary.meaning: A solution that cannot hold any more dissolved solid.
vocabulary.example: We then say the solution is saturated - it is full and cannot hold any more.

vocabulary.meaning: Separating an undissolved solid from a liquid using a filter.
vocabulary.example: Evaporation gets a dissolved solid back; filtration only catches undissolved solids.

vocabulary.meaning: A liquid turning to gas, leaving a dissolved solid behind.
vocabulary.example: Evaporation gets a dissolved solid back; filtration only catches undissolved solids.

commonMistakes: It is not seen, but it is still there.
Calling every change that looks different a chemical change.
Thinking melting and dissolving are the same.

fluency.prompt: Which of these is a reversible change? (a) Burning charcoal (b) Melting ghee (c) Frying an egg (d) Rusting a nail
fluency.answer: (b) Melting ghee. Melting only changes the state; the ghee can be cooled back to solid, so it is reversible. The others all make new materials.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) Melting ghee. Melting only changes the state; the ghee can be cooled back to solid, so it is reversible. The others all make new materials.

fluency.answer: (a) Water and oxygen. Iron rusts only when both water and oxygen from the air can reach it.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

fluency.answer: (d) All of these. A new colour, a new gas, and heat or light are all clues that a chemical change has happened.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (d) All of these. A new colour, a new gas, and heat or light are all clues that a chemical change has happened.

fluency.prompt: Which is a pure substance? (a) Seawater (b) Air (c) Clean water (d) Rice with stones
fluency.answer: (c) Clean water. Pure water is made of only one kind of material. Seawater, air, and rice with stones are all mixtures.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (c) Clean water. Pure water is made of only one kind of material. Seawater, air, and rice with stones are all mixtures.

fluency.prompt: Which makes sugar dissolve faster? (a) Colder water (b) Bigger lumps (c) Stirring (d) Less water
fluency.answer: (c) Stirring. Stirring, heating, and using smaller pieces all speed up dissolving.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (c) Stirring. Stirring, heating, and using smaller pieces all speed up dissolving.

fluency.prompt: A mixture where you cannot see the separate parts, like salt water, is called: (a) heterogeneous (b) homogeneous (c) irreversible (d) magnetic
fluency.answer: (b) Homogeneous. In a homogeneous mixture the parts are spread evenly and cannot be seen separately, like salt water.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) Homogeneous. In a homogeneous mixture the parts are spread evenly and cannot be seen separately, like salt water.

realProblems.prompt: Use what you have learned to answer these real-life questions.
realProblems.answer: Coastal air is damp and salty, and salt and moisture speed up rusting, so metal near the sea rusts faster. Idris could paint the gate or coat it with oil or grease to keep water and oxygen away from the iron.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Coastal air is damp and salty, and salt and moisture speed up rusting, so metal near the sea rusts faster. Idris could paint the gate or coat it with oil or grease to keep water and oxygen away from the iron.

realProblems.prompt: Idris lives near the coast and his metal gate keeps rusting. Explain why coastal metal rusts faster, and suggest two things he could do to protect the gate.
realProblems.answer: Hani should stir the water and grind the sugar into smaller pieces. Stirring keeps fresh water moving past the sugar, and smaller pieces give the water more surface to dissolve, so both speed up dissolving without heat.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Hani should stir the water and grind the sugar into smaller pieces. Stirring keeps fresh water moving past the sugar, and smaller pieces give the water more surface to dissolve, so both speed up dissolving without heat.

realProblems.prompt: Hani wants her sugar to dissolve quickly in cold water because there is no time to heat it. Give her two tips that do not need heat, and explain why each works.
realProblems.answer: First filter the water through cloth or sand to remove the mud, then boil it to kill germs. Filtering removes the solid dirt you can see, but only boiling makes the water safe by killing the tiny germs a filter cannot catch.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: First filter the water through cloth or sand to remove the mud, then boil it to kill germs. Filtering removes the solid dirt you can see, but only boiling makes the water safe by killing the tiny germs a filter cannot catch.

realProblems.prompt: A family has muddy river water. Describe the steps they should take to make it clean and safe to drink, and explain why each step matters.
realProblems.answer: Use a magnet to remove the iron nails, add water to dissolve the salt and filter out the sand, then evaporate the salty water to leave pure salt. Each step uses a property: iron is magnetic, sand does not dissolve, and salt is left when water evaporates.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Use a magnet to remove the iron nails, add water to dissolve the salt and filter out the sand, then evaporate the salty water to leave pure salt. Each step uses a property: iron is magnetic, sand does not dissolve, and salt is left when water evaporates.

realProblems.prompt: Sagal accidentally mixes salt, sand, and iron nails in one bowl. Describe a full plan to separate all three, naming the method for each.
realProblems.answer: Cooking is an irreversible chemical change. When the batter is heated, new materials form that are firm and spongy, and there is no way to turn these new materials back into the runny batter we started with.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Cooking is an irreversible chemical change. When the batter is heated, new materials form that are firm and spongy, and there is no way to turn these new materials back into the runny batter we started with.

realProblems.prompt: Explain to a younger child why cooked canjeero can never turn back into runny batter.
realProblems.answer: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between a reversible and an irreversible change?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A reversible change can be undone so you get the original material back (like melting ice), while an irreversible change cannot be undone and usually makes a new material (like baking bread).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name three clues that tell you a chemical change has happened.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Any three of: a colour change, a gas being made, heat or light given out, a new smell, or a change that will not reverse.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain why rusting is an irreversible change.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Rusting is irreversible because the iron joins with oxygen and water to make a new material, iron oxide, which has different properties and cannot be turned back into iron.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between a mixture and a solution?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A mixture is any two or more materials jumbled together; a solution is a special mixture where a solid has dissolved evenly into a liquid so it cannot be seen.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the solute, solvent, and solution when you stir salt into water.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The solute is the salt, the solvent is the water, and the solution is the salt water they make together.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: List the three things that make a solid dissolve faster.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Heating the liquid, stirring it, and breaking the solid into smaller pieces.

questions.question: Which of these is a reversible change?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Melting ghee. Melting only changes the state; the ghee can be cooled back to solid, so it is reversible. The others all make new materials.

questions.question: Which is an irreversible change?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Baking canjeero. Cooking makes a new material that cannot turn back into batter. Freezing, dissolving, and melting can all be reversed.

questions.question: When salt dissolves in water, the salt:
options: spreads out between the water particles
questions.answer: spreads out between the water particles
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: It spreads out between the water particles. The salt is still there, hidden among the water particles, which is why the water tastes salty and the salt returns when the water evaporates.

questions.question: In a cup of sweet shaah, the sugar is the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Solute. The solute is the solid that dissolves - here, the sugar.

questions.question: In salt water, the water is the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Solvent. The solvent is the liquid that does the dissolving - here, the water.

questions.question: Which tool would separate iron filings from sand?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: A magnet. Iron is magnetic, so a magnet pulls the iron filings out while leaving the sand behind.

questions.question: Which method separates salt from salt water?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Evaporation. Heating the salt water lets the water escape as gas and leaves the salt behind. A magnet and sieve would not work, and a filter cannot catch dissolved salt.

questions.question: Iron needs which two things to rust?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Water and oxygen. Iron rusts only when both water and oxygen from the air can reach it.

questions.question: Which of these is a clue that a chemical change has happened?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: All of these. A new colour, a new gas, and heat or light are all clues that a chemical change has happened.

questions.question: Which is a pure substance?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Clean water. Pure water is made of only one kind of material. Seawater, air, and rice with stones are all mixtures.

questions.question: Which makes sugar dissolve faster?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Stirring. Stirring, heating, and using smaller pieces all speed up dissolving.

questions.question: A mixture where you cannot see the separate parts, like salt water, is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Homogeneous. In a homogeneous mixture the parts are spread evenly and cannot be seen separately, like salt water.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, you will be able to:
I can tell the difference between a reversible change and an irreversible change, and give clear examples of each from your own life.
I can explain the difference between a physical change and a chemical change, and describe the evidence that a new material has been made.
I can describe what mixtures and solutions are, and how they are different from pure substances.
I can investigate and choose the right method to separate a mixture, such as filtering, evaporating, sieving, or using a magnet.
I can use particle theory - the idea that everything is made of tiny particles - to explain melting, dissolving, and other changes.
I can identify the factors that make a solid dissolve faster, such as heat, stirring, and grinding into smaller pieces.
I can connect what you learn to real life in your community, from rusting roofs to making yoghurt and collecting salt.
